summary: This notebook demonstrates how to perform inference for RNA 3D structure prediction using a pretrained RibonanzaNet model. It tokenizes RNA sequences, passes them through a custom finetuned architecture that outputs 3D atomic coordinates, and applies test-time averaging to stabilize predictions. The author explicitly shows how to toggle `model.train()` during inference to leverage dropout for stochastic prediction averaging. Readers learn how to adapt a pretrained sequence-to-structure model for submission generation and visualize the resulting 3D conformations.
competition_flow: Loads test RNA sequences, tokenizes them, runs them through a pretrained RibonanzaNet model with a 3D coordinate prediction head, averages predictions across 5 stochastic forward passes, and exports the coordinates to a CSV submission file.
data_processing: Applies test-time averaging via 5 forward passes (4 with dropout enabled, 1 with it disabled) to stabilize predictions
models: RibonanzaNet (finetuned with a linear 3D coordinate predictor head)
ensembling: Averages predictions across 5 forward passes per sequence, using 4 stochastic passes (with dropout active) and 1 deterministic pass to mitigate variance.
insights: Using `model.train()` during inference enables dropout-based test-time averaging, which can stabilize predictions for stochastic models.
Directly predicting 3D coordinates from sequence embeddings bypasses the need for intermediate contact map or pairwise feature extraction during inference.
